params.text:
What gets in the way

Even with the best intentions, many things can block communication. Recognising barriers is the first step to removing them. Barriers can come from the surroundings, from a person's health, or from feelings and circumstances.

  • Environmental: noise, poor lighting, no privacy or a busy space.
  • Physical and sensory: hearing or sight loss, pain, tiredness or the effects of a stroke.
  • Language: a different first language, jargon, or unfamiliar words.
  • Emotional: anxiety, low mood, embarrassment or feeling rushed.

Often several barriers combine at once. A tired person in a noisy day room, who is also in some pain, faces three obstacles at the same time, and each one makes the others harder to overcome.

Why barriers matter

When a barrier is present, messages get missed or misread. A person with hearing loss may nod politely without catching a word. Someone in pain or distress may not take in your explanation. A jargon-filled sentence can leave a person too embarrassed to admit they did not understand, so they simply agree.

Barriers are not the person's fault, and they are rarely permanent. Your job is to spot them and adapt. Notice when someone seems to be struggling, ask yourself what might be getting in the way, and change the environment or your approach. A quiet, unhurried, well-lit space with your full attention removes many barriers before they start.

params.question: A noisy, poorly lit room is an example of an environmental barrier to communication.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: This is actually true. Noise and poor lighting are environmental barriers — changing the setting can remove them.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Noise, poor lighting and lack of privacy are all environmental barriers that you can often reduce.
